questions.question: Explain the algorithm to find the middle element of a singly linked list in a single pass. What are the time and space complexities?
keyPoints: Use slow and fast pointers
Slow moves one node, fast moves two nodes
When fast reaches end, slow is at middle
Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

questions.question: Describe Floyd's cycle detection algorithm. How does it detect a cycle in a linked list?
keyPoints: Two pointers - slow and fast
Slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps
If they meet, cycle exists
Also called tortoise and hare algorithm

questions.question: How do you find the nth node from the end of a linked list in one traversal? Explain with example.
keyPoints: Use two pointers technique
Move first pointer n nodes ahead
Then move both pointers together
When first reaches end, second is at nth from end

questions.question: What is a circular linked list? Differentiate between singly and doubly circular linked lists.
keyPoints: Last node points to first node instead of NULL
Singly circular: only next pointers form circle
Doubly circular: both next and prev pointers form circle
Traversal requires stopping condition

questions.question: Explain how sparse matrices are represented using linked lists. Why is this representation efficient?
keyPoints: Only non-zero elements are stored
Each node contains row, column, value
Separate linked lists for rows and columns
Saves memory when most elements are zero

questions.question: Describe the procedure to reverse a singly linked list iteratively. What is the space complexity?
keyPoints: Use three pointers: prev, current, next
Initialize prev=NULL, current=head
Reverse links while traversing
Space complexity: O(1)

questions.question: Explain the merge operation for two sorted linked lists. What is the time complexity?
keyPoints: Compare heads of both lists
Pick smaller element and attach to result
Continue until one list exhausts
Attach remaining list. Time: O(m+n)

questions.question: Write an algorithm to detect and find the starting node of a cycle in a linked list. Explain the mathematical relationship used.
keyPoints: Use Floyd's cycle detection
If cycle exists, find meeting point
Move one pointer to head, keep other at meeting
Move both one step at a time
They meet at cycle start
Mathematical proof: distance from head to cycle start equals distance from meeting point to cycle start

questions.question: Explain polynomial representation using linked lists. Write an algorithm to add two polynomials represented as linked lists.
keyPoints: Each term stored as node with coefficient and exponent
Terms sorted by exponent (descending)
Algorithm: traverse both lists
Compare exponents, add coefficients if equal
Insert term if exponent unique
Time complexity: O(m+n)

questions.question: How would you find the intersection point of two linked lists? Explain with algorithm and analyze its complexity.
keyPoints: Method 1: Calculate lengths of both lists
Find difference d
Advance longer list by d
Traverse both together until addresses match
Time: O(m+n), Space: O(1)
Method 2: Hash set approach: O(m+n) time, O(m) or O(n) space

questions.question: Explain the concept of a header node (sentinel node) in linked lists. How does it simplify insertion and deletion operations? Write code for insertion at the beginning using header node.
keyPoints: Header node is dummy node before first data node
Head always points to header
No special case for empty list
No need to update head pointer for first element insertion
Simplifies boundary conditions
